news_summary: Today's space news highlights significant advancements as NASA launches initiatives to fulfill national space goals, develops technology to discover Earth-like exoplanets and explore the Moon's south pole, while supercomputers solve a long-standing mystery about giant stars.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures : la NASA lance des initiatives visant à atteindre les objectifs spatiaux nationaux, développe des technologies pour découvrir des exoplanètes semblables à la Terre et explorer le pôle sud de la Lune, tandis que des supercalculateurs élucident un mystère de longue date concernant les étoiles géantes.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan avances significativos: la NASA pone en marcha iniciativas para alcanzar los objetivos espaciales nacionales, desarrolla tecnología para descubrir exoplanetas similares a la Tierra y explorar el polo sur de la Luna, mientras que los superordenadores resuelven un misterio que llevaba mucho tiempo sin resolverse sobre las estrellas gigantes.

articles.title: NASA Unveils Initiatives to Achieve America’s National Space Policy
articles.summary: As part of its “Ignition” event on Tuesday, NASA announced a series of transformative agencywide initiatives designed to achieve President Donald J. Trump’s National Space Policy and advance American leadership in space. These actions reflect the urgency of the moment, but also the tremendous opportunity ahead for world-changing science and discovery. “NASA is committed to […]
articles.content:
Artist’s concept of Phase 3 of NASA’s Moon Base. Credit: NASA

As part of its “Ignition” event on Tuesday, NASA announced a series of transformative agencywide initiatives designed to achieve President Donald J. Trump’s National Space Policy and advance American leadership in space. These actions reflect the urgency of the moment, but also the tremendous opportunity ahead for world-changing science and discovery.

“NASA is committed to achieving the near‑impossible once again, to return to the Moon before the end of President Trump’s term, build a Moon base, establish an enduring presence, and do the other things needed to ensure American leadership in space. This is why it is essential we leave an event like Ignition with complete alignment on the national imperative that is our collective mission. The clock is running in this great‑power competition, and success or failure will be measured in months, not years,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman. “If we concentrate NASA’s extraordinary resources on the objectives of the National Space Policy, clear away needless obstacles that impede progress, and unleash the workforce and industrial might of our nation and partners, then returning to the Moon and building a base will seem pale in comparison to what we will be capable of accomplishing in the years ahead.”

NASA Associate Administrator Amit Kshatriya said, “Today we are aligning NASA around the mission. On the Moon, we are shifting to a focused, phased architecture that builds capability landing by landing, incrementally, and in alignment with our industrial and international partners. In low Earth orbit (LEO), we are recognizing where the market is and where it isn’t, recognizing the incredible value of the International Space Station, and building a transition that builds a competitive commercial ecosystem rather than forcing a single outcome the market cannot support. In our science missions, we are opening the lunar surface to researchers and students nationwide, and with Space Reactor‑1 Freedom, we are finally putting nuclear propulsion on a trajectory out of the laboratory and into deep space. And this is all possible by investing in our people, bringing critical skills back into the agency, putting our teams where the machines are being built, and creating real pathways for the next generation of NASA leaders. Our workforce is the jewel of NASA, and from their leaders, they need clear mission goals, the tools to execute, and to get out of their way. This is what Ignition is about.”

Going back to the Moon

The announcements build on recent updates to the Artemis program, including standardizing the SLS (Space Launch System) rocket configuration, adding an additional mission in 2027, and undertaking at least one surface landing every year thereafter. Under this previously updated architecture, Artemis III – scheduled for 2027 – will focus on testing integrated systems and operational capabilities in Earth orbit in advance of the Artemis IV lunar landing.

Looking beyond Artemis V, NASA announced March 24 it will begin to incorporate more commercially procured and reusable hardware to undertake frequent and affordable crewed missions to the lunar surface, initially targeting landings every six months, with the potential to increase cadence as capabilities mature.

To achieve an enduring human presence on the Moon, NASA also announced a phased approach to building a lunar base. As part of this strategy, the agency intends to pause Gateway in its current form and shift focus to infrastructure that enables sustained surface operations. Despite challenges with some existing hardware, the agency will repurpose applicable equipment and leverage international partner commitments to support these objectives.

In the coming days, NASA will release Requests for Information (RFIs) and draft Requests for Proposals (RFPs) to ensure continued progress in meeting national objectives.

Building the Moon Base

NASA’s plan for establishing a sustained lunar presence will roll out in three deliberate phases.

Phase One: Build, Test, Learn

NASA shifts from bespoke, infrequent missions to a repeatable, modular approach. Through CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) deliveries and the LTV (Lunar Terrain Vehicle) program, the agency will increase the tempo of lunar activity, sending rovers, instruments, and technology demonstrations that advance mobility, power generation (including radioisotope heater units and radioisotope thermoelectric generators), communications, navigation, surface operations, and a wide range of scientific investigations.

Phase Two: Establish Early Infrastructure

With lessons from early missions in hand, NASA moves toward semi‑habitable infrastructure and regular logistics. This phase supports recurring astronaut operations on the surface and incorporates major international contributions, including JAXA’s (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) pressurized rover, and potentially other partner scientific payloads, rovers, and infrastructure/transportation capabilities.

Phase Three: Enable Long‑Duration Human Presence

As cargo‑capable human landing systems (HLS) come online, NASA will deliver heavier infrastructure needed for a continuous human foothold on the Moon, marking the transition from periodic expeditions to a permanent lunar base. This will include ASI’s (Italian Space Agency) Multi-purpose Habitats (MPH), CSA’s (Canadian Space Agency) Lunar Utility Vehicle, and opportunities for additional contributions in habitation, surface mobility and logistics.

Ensuring American presence in low Earth orbit

While building a sustainable lunar architecture, NASA is also reaffirming its commitment to low Earth orbit. For more than two decades, the International Space Station has served as a world‑class orbital laboratory, enabling more than 4,000 research investigations, supporting more than 5,000 researchers, and hosting visitors from 26 countries. The space station required 37 shuttle flights, 160 spacewalks, two decades, and more than $100 billion to design, develop, and build. The orbital laboratory cannot operate indefinitely. The transition to commercial stations must be thoughtful, deliberate, and structured to support long‑term industry success.

NASA is introducing and seeking industry feedback on an additional LEO strategy that preserves all current pathways while adding a phased, International Space Station‑anchored approach to avoid any gap in U.S. human presence and mature a robust commercial ecosystem. Under this alternative approach, NASA would procure a government‑owned Core Module that attaches to the space station, followed by commercial modules that are validated using International Space Station capabilities and later detach into free flight. After maturing technical and operational capabilities and market demand is realized, the stations would detach and NASA would be one of many customers purchasing commercial services. To stimulate the orbital economy, NASA would expand industry opportunities, including private astronaut missions, commander seat sales, joint missions, multiple module competitions, and prize‑based awards.

An industry RFI opens Wednesday, March 25, to inform partnership structures, financing, and risk mitigation.

Advancing world-changing discovery with current, developing science missions

In a Golden Age of exploration and discovery, NASA takes full advantage of every opportunity to get science into space. The James Webb Space Telescope continues to transform our understanding of the early universe, Parker Solar Probe has flown through the atmosphere of the Sun, NASA has shown it can defend the planet by deflecting asteroids, and Earth science data is used extensively by American companies, U.S. agriculture, and disaster relief. On the International Space Station, NASA is conducting groundbreaking experiments in quantum science.

Future opportunities will advance U.S. leadership in space science. The Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, launching as early as this fall, will advance our understanding of dark energy, and has created a new standard for the management of large science missions. Dragonfly will launch a nuclear-powered octocopter in 2028, arriving at Saturn’s moon Titan in 2034 to explore its complex, organic-rich environment. In 2028, NASA will launch and deliver ESA’s (European Space Agency) Rosalind Franklin Rover to Mars, with NASA’s contributed mass spectrometer for the Mars Organic Molecule Analyzer (MOMA) instrument, which may result in the most advanced detection and analysis of organic matter ever conducted on Mars. A new Earth science mission launching next year will measure for the first time the evolution of the dynamics within convective storms to improve the prediction of extreme weather events up to six hours before the storm occurs.

The agency detailed how advancements in lunar science also will be afforded by the build out of the Moon Base and underpin future Moon and Mars exploration. With an accelerated CLPS cadence, targeting up to 30 robotic landings starting in 2027, NASA is expediting delivery of science and technology to the lunar surface. There will be many opportunities for payload delivery including rovers, hoppers, and drones with contributions welcomed from industry, academia, and international partners. Near-term payloads include the VIPER rover and the LuSEE‑Night mission. An RFI will be released March 24 that calls for payloads capable of supporting NASA’s science and technology goals for additional 2027 and 2028 flights. It will enable students and researchers across the country to work on scientific instruments for use on the surface of the Moon in the years ahead. This RFI also will solicit payloads incorporated on future missions to Mars including the Mars Telecom Network (MTN) and a nuclear technology demonstration mission.

The agency intends to partner with philanthropic and privately funded research organizations with shared objectives in space science.

Other RFIs released March 24 will strengthen “Science as a Service” partnerships and commercial capabilities, allowing NASA to streamline legacy operations and focus investment on the transformational missions only the agency can lead.

Finally, NASA will unveil a previously unseen pair of images from the James Webb and Hubble Space Telescopes. These images show the planet Saturn in unprecedented detail in both infrared and visible wavelengths.

America underway on nuclear power in space

In addition to these scientific missions, after decades of study and in response to the National Space Policy, NASA announced a major step forward in bringing nuclear power and propulsion from the lab to space.

NASA will launch the Space Reactor‑1 Freedom, the first nuclear powered interplanetary spacecraft, to Mars before the end of 2028, demonstrating advanced nuclear electric propulsion in deep space. Nuclear electric propulsion provides an extraordinary capability for efficient mass transport in deep space and enables high power missions beyond Jupiter where solar arrays are not effective.

When SR-1 Freedom reaches Mars, it will deploy the Skyfall payload of Ingenuity‑class helicopters to continue exploring the Red Planet. SR-1 Freedom will establish flight heritage nuclear hardware, set regulatory and launch precedent, and activate the industrial base for future fission power systems across propulsion, surface, and long‑duration missions. NASA and its U.S. Department of Energy partner will unlock the capabilities required for sustained exploration beyond the Moon and eventual journeys to Mars and the outer solar system.

None of these endeavors can succeed without the NASA workforce. As previously announced, the agency is rebuilding its core competencies, converting thousands of contractor positions to civil service, and restoring the engineering, technical, and operational strengths expected of the world’s premier space organization.

NASA is expanding opportunities for interns and early‑career professionals and, in partnership with the U.S. Office of Personnel Management and NASA Force, is creating new pathways for experienced industry talent to serve through term‑based appointments. The agency also is seeking to open opportunities for NASA employees to gain valuable experience working within the most technologically advanced space industry in history.

The changes announced on March 24 will be implemented during the coming months, with teams agencywide ensuring a smooth transition while advancing key programs and partnerships.

NASA will embed subject‑matter experts across the supply chain – at every major vendor, subcontractor, and critical‑path component – to challenge assumptions, solve problems, accelerate production, and help ensure the right outcomes are achieved.

Through these reforms, NASA is strengthening its ability to deliver on the President’s National Space Policy and ensure continued American superiority in space.

Learn more about NASA’s Ignition news online:

https://www.nasa.gov/ignition

-end-

Camille Gallo / George Alderman

Headquarters, Washington

202-358-1600

camille.m.gallo@nasa.gov / george.a.alderman@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article details NASA's comprehensive and transformative initiatives to advance American leadership in space, including returning to the Moon, building a sustained lunar base, advancing nuclear propulsion, and fostering commercial and international partnerships. These efforts represent a significant, tangible advancement in space exploration with broad scientific, technological, and societal benefits, supported by detailed phased plans and workforce development.
articles.personality_title: NASA launches bold plans to return to the Moon and build a lasting base
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working to follow the United States’ National Space Policy by advancing American leadership in space. This includes returning humans to the Moon and developing new technologies for space exploration.

**What happened** – At the “Ignition” event on March 24, NASA announced new projects to return astronauts to the Moon by 2027, build a permanent Moon base in three phases, and develop nuclear-powered spacecraft for deep space missions. The agency also shared plans to support commercial space stations in low Earth orbit and expand scientific research with new missions like the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope and the Dragonfly mission to Titan, Saturn’s moon.

**Impact** – These plans mark a clear step toward long-term human presence on the Moon, using reusable technology and international partnerships. NASA is shifting from one-time missions to a steady rhythm of landings and building infrastructure that can support astronauts over time. The introduction of nuclear propulsion will allow faster and more powerful space travel beyond the Moon, opening new possibilities for exploring Mars and beyond.

**What's next step** – NASA will soon request proposals from companies and researchers to help build the Moon base and develop new technologies. The agency will also continue strengthening its workforce by hiring more skilled workers and creating opportunities for young professionals. Additional robotic missions will deliver scientific instruments to the lunar surface starting in 2027.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s new initiatives aim to establish a steady human presence on the Moon, advance deep space travel with nuclear power, and expand scientific exploration through partnerships and technology development.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA lance des plans audacieux pour retourner sur la Lune et construire une base durable
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA travaille à suivre la politique spatiale nationale des États-Unis en renforçant le leadership américain dans l’espace. Cela comprend le retour des astronautes sur la Lune et le développement de nouvelles technologies pour l’exploration spatiale.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors de l’événement « Ignition » du 24 mars, la NASA a annoncé de nouveaux projets pour ramener des astronautes sur la Lune d’ici 2027, construire une base lunaire permanente en trois phases et développer des engins spatiaux à propulsion nucléaire pour les missions dans l’espace lointain. L’agence a également partagé ses plans pour soutenir les stations spatiales commerciales en orbite basse et étendre la recherche scientifique avec de nouvelles missions comme le télescope spatial Nancy Grace Roman et la mission Dragonfly vers Titan, la lune de Saturne.

**Impact** – Ces plans marquent une étape claire vers une présence humaine à long terme sur la Lune, en utilisant des technologies réutilisables et des partenariats internationaux. La NASA passe de missions ponctuelles à un rythme régulier d’atterrissages et à la construction d’infrastructures capables de soutenir les astronautes sur la durée. L’introduction de la propulsion nucléaire permettra des voyages spatiaux plus rapides et plus puissants au-delà de la Lune, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités d’exploration de Mars et au-delà.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA demandera bientôt des propositions aux entreprises et aux chercheurs pour aider à construire la base lunaire et développer de nouvelles technologies. L’agence continuera également à renforcer son personnel en recrutant davantage de travailleurs qualifiés et en créant des opportunités pour les jeunes professionnels. Des missions robotiques supplémentaires livreront des instruments scientifiques sur la surface lunaire à partir de 2027.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les nouvelles initiatives de la NASA visent à établir une présence humaine régulière sur la Lune, à faire progresser les voyages dans l’espace profond grâce à la propulsion nucléaire et à étendre l’exploration scientifique grâce à des partenariats et au développement technologique.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA lanza planes audaces para regresar a la Luna y construir una base permanente
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA trabaja para cumplir con la Política Nacional Espacial de Estados Unidos, fortaleciendo el liderazgo americano en el espacio. Esto incluye regresar humanos a la Luna y desarrollar nuevas tecnologías para la exploración espacial.

**Qué sucedió** – En el evento “Ignition” del 24 de marzo, la NASA anunció nuevos proyectos para llevar astronautas a la Luna para 2027, construir una base lunar permanente en tres fases y desarrollar naves espaciales con propulsión nuclear para misiones en el espacio profundo. La agencia también compartió planes para apoyar estaciones espaciales comerciales en órbita terrestre baja y ampliar la investigación científica con nuevas misiones como el telescopio espacial Nancy Grace Roman y la misión Dragonfly a Titán, luna de Saturno.

**Impacto** – Estos planes representan un paso claro hacia una presencia humana a largo plazo en la Luna, usando tecnología reutilizable y asociaciones internacionales. La NASA cambia de misiones puntuales a un ritmo constante de aterrizajes y construcción de infraestructura que apoye a los astronautas con el tiempo. La introducción de la propulsión nuclear permitirá viajes espaciales más rápidos y potentes más allá de la Luna, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para explorar Marte y más allá.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA solicitará pronto propuestas a empresas e investigadores para ayudar a construir la base lunar y desarrollar nuevas tecnologías. La agencia también fortalecerá su equipo contratando más trabajadores capacitados y creando oportunidades para jóvenes profesionales. Misiones robóticas adicionales entregarán instrumentos científicos en la superficie lunar a partir de 2027.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las nuevas iniciativas de la NASA buscan establecer una presencia humana constante en la Luna, avanzar en los viajes espaciales profundos con energía nuclear y ampliar la exploración científica mediante asociaciones y desarrollo tecnológico.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a futuristic lunar base under a starry sky, showing modular habitats, rovers, and solar panels gently illuminated by Earthlight, with symbolic silhouettes of interconnected international spacecraft orbiting above and a glowing nucleus representing nuclear propulsion powering the scene’s progress and exploration.

articles.title: Supercomputers just solved a 50-year-old mystery about giant stars
articles.summary: Astronomers have finally cracked a decades-old mystery about red giant stars—how material from their deep interiors makes its way to the surface. Using cutting-edge supercomputer simulations, researchers discovered that stellar rotation plays a powerful role in mixing elements across a previously unexplained barrier inside the star.
articles.content:
Recent advances in supercomputing have allowed scientists to tackle a long-standing question in astronomy. Researchers have been trying to understand why the chemical makeup at the surface of red giant stars changes as these stars evolve.

For many years, scientists struggled to connect what happens deep inside a red giant to what is observed at its surface. Nuclear reactions in the core alter the star's internal composition, but a stable layer separates this region from the outer convective envelope. How material manages to cross this barrier and reach the surface remained unclear.

In a new study published in Nature Astronomy, researchers from the University of Victoria's (UVic) Astronomy Research Centre (ARC) and the University of Minnesota have now found the answer.

Stellar Rotation Drives Element Mixing

The key factor is stellar rotation.

"Using high-resolution 3D simulations, we were able to identify the impact that the rotation of these stars was having on the ability for elements to cross the barrier," says Simon Blouin, lead researcher and postdoctoral fellow at UVic. "Stellar rotation is crucial and provides a natural explanation for the observed chemical signatures in typical red giants. This discovery is another step forward in understanding how stars evolve."

Scientists have long known that stars like our Sun expand dramatically once they run out of hydrogen in their cores, becoming red giants that can grow up to 100 times their original size. Since the 1970s, astronomers have detected changes in their surface chemistry during this phase, including shifts in carbon-12 to carbon-13 ratios. These changes suggest that material from deep inside the star must be transported outward, but the exact mechanism had not been confirmed.

"We knew that internal waves, generated by churning motions in the convective envelope, were able to pass through this barrier layer, but previous simulations found that these waves transported very little material. We were able to show that the rotation of the star dramatically amplifies how effectively these waves can mix material across the barrier, to an extent that matches the observed changes in surface composition," explained Blouin.

Blouin and his colleagues found that rotation can boost mixing rates by more than 100 times compared to stars that are not rotating. Faster rotation leads to even stronger mixing. Because our Sun will eventually become a red giant, these findings also provide insight into its future evolution.

Advanced Simulations Reveal Hidden Processes

To uncover this process, the team relied on hydrodynamical simulations, which model how material flows inside stars in three dimensions. These simulations are extremely complex and require powerful computing systems, making the discovery possible only with recent advances in supercomputing.

"Until recently, while stellar rotation was thought to be part of solving this conundrum, limited computing abilities prevented us from quantitatively testing the hypothesis," says Falk Herwig, principal investigator and director of ARC. "These simulations allow us to tease out small effects to determine what actually happens, helping us to understand our observations."

The researchers used computing resources from the Texas Advanced Computing Centre at the University of Texas at Austin and the Trillium supercomputing cluster at SciNet at the University of Toronto. Trillium, launched in August 2025, is among the most powerful systems available in Canada for large-scale academic simulations and is part of the Digital Research Alliance of Canada. Its enhanced processing capabilities played a crucial role in enabling this work.

"We were able to discover a new stellar mixing process only because of the immense computing power of the new Trillium machine. These are the computationally most intensive stellar convection and internal gravity wave simulations performed to date, " said Herwig.

Broader Impact and Future Research

The methods used in this study extend beyond astrophysics. The same computational approaches can help scientists better understand fluid motion in many systems, including ocean currents, atmospheric patterns, and blood flow. Herwig is collaborating with researchers in these areas to build shared tools and infrastructure for large-scale simulations.

Blouin plans to continue exploring how stellar rotation affects different types of stars. Future work will examine how varying rotation patterns influence mixing efficiency and whether similar processes occur in other stages of stellar evolution.

This research was supported by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council (NSERC), the National Science Foundation (NSF) and the US Department of Energy.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding stellar evolution by solving a 50-year-old mystery about red giant stars using advanced supercomputer simulations. This discovery has broad implications for astronomy and related fields, providing meaningful insight into stellar processes and future research directions. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights the use of cutting-edge technology to achieve a major advancement in space science.
articles.personality_title: Supercomputers reveal how giant stars mix their elements
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For decades, astronomers have wondered how red giant stars change their surface chemistry as they grow. These stars expand hugely after using up their core hydrogen, but a barrier inside them made it unclear how material from deep inside could reach the surface.

**What happened** – Scientists from the University of Victoria and the University of Minnesota used powerful 3D supercomputer simulations to study red giants. They discovered that the stars’ rotation helps mix elements across the internal barrier. This mixing explains the changes in surface chemicals observed since the 1970s.

**Impact** – This finding solves a 50-year-old puzzle by showing that rotation boosts material mixing by over 100 times compared to non-rotating stars. It also gives new clues about how our Sun will change when it becomes a red giant. The research relied on Canada’s most advanced supercomputer, Trillium, highlighting how new technology can unlock hidden star processes.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will explore how different rotation speeds affect mixing in other types of stars and stages of their life cycles. The simulation methods may also help study fluid flows in oceans, atmospheres, and even blood, showing the wide value of this work.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using supercomputers, scientists have shown that rotation in red giant stars drives material mixing, solving a long-standing mystery about how their surfaces change over time.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les superordinateurs révèlent comment les étoiles géantes mélangent leurs éléments
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des décennies, les astronomes se demandent comment les étoiles géantes rouges changent la composition chimique de leur surface en grandissant. Ces étoiles s’agrandissent énormément après avoir épuisé l’hydrogène de leur cœur, mais une barrière à l’intérieur empêchait de comprendre comment le matériel profond pouvait atteindre la surface.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Victoria et de l’Université du Minnesota ont utilisé des simulations 3D puissantes sur superordinateurs pour étudier les géantes rouges. Ils ont découvert que la rotation des étoiles aide à mélanger les éléments à travers cette barrière interne. Ce mélange explique les changements chimiques observés à la surface depuis les années 1970.

**Impact** – Cette découverte résout une énigme vieille de 50 ans en montrant que la rotation augmente le mélange des matériaux par plus de 100 fois comparé aux étoiles non tournantes. Elle donne aussi des indices sur l’évolution future de notre Soleil. L’étude a utilisé Trillium, le superordinateur le plus avancé du Canada, montrant comment la technologie permet de découvrir des processus cachés dans les étoiles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs étudieront comment différentes vitesses de rotation influencent le mélange dans d’autres types d’étoiles et à différents stades. Les méthodes de simulation pourront aussi aider à comprendre les mouvements de fluides dans les océans, l’atmosphère, et même le sang, montrant l’utilité large de ce travail.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Grâce aux superordinateurs, les scientifiques ont montré que la rotation des géantes rouges provoque le mélange des éléments, résolvant un mystère ancien sur l’évolution de leur surface.
articles.personality_title_es: Supercomputadoras revelan cómo las estrellas gigantes mezclan sus elementos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante décadas, los astrónomos se han preguntado cómo las estrellas gigantes rojas cambian la química de su superficie al crecer. Estas estrellas se expanden mucho después de agotar el hidrógeno en su núcleo, pero una barrera interna impedía entender cómo el material profundo llegaba a la superficie.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Victoria y la Universidad de Minnesota usaron simulaciones 3D muy potentes en supercomputadoras para estudiar las gigantes rojas. Descubrieron que la rotación de las estrellas ayuda a mezclar elementos a través de esa barrera interna. Esta mezcla explica los cambios químicos observados en la superficie desde los años 70.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo resuelve un misterio de 50 años al mostrar que la rotación aumenta la mezcla de material más de 100 veces en comparación con estrellas sin rotación. También da pistas sobre cómo cambiará nuestro Sol cuando se convierta en gigante roja. El estudio utilizó Trillium, la supercomputadora más avanzada de Canadá, demostrando cómo la tecnología puede revelar procesos ocultos en las estrellas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores explorarán cómo diferentes velocidades de rotación afectan la mezcla en otros tipos de estrellas y etapas. Los métodos de simulación también pueden ayudar a estudiar movimientos de fluidos en océanos, atmósferas e incluso sangre, mostrando el alcance amplio de este trabajo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usando supercomputadoras, los científicos demostraron que la rotación en estrellas gigantes rojas impulsa la mezcla de materiales, resolviendo un misterio antiguo sobre cómo cambia su superficie con el tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, glowing red giant star with swirling bands of vibrant colors inside symbolizing internal rotation, where luminous streams of particles spiral upward through a semi-transparent layered barrier toward the star’s bright surface, set against a simple dark space background with subtle hints of a supercomputer’s grid-like energy pattern illuminating the scene.

articles.title: NASA Research Proposes Technology to Seek Earth-Like Exoplanets
articles.summary: As NASA seeks to understand the mysteries of the universe, the agency is advancing technologies to locate and explore Earth-like planets far beyond our solar system. A key element of this research involves observing reflected light from exoplanets, which can reveal indicators of Earth-like features such as water and oxygen. However, detecting this faint reflected […]
articles.content:
As NASA seeks to understand the mysteries of the universe, the agency is advancing technologies to locate and explore Earth-like planets far beyond our solar system. A key element of this research involves observing reflected light from exoplanets, which can reveal indicators of Earth-like features such as water and oxygen. However, detecting this faint reflected light with current telescope technology remains a significant challenge due to the overwhelming brightness of nearby stars and other celestial objects.

NASA’s Hybrid Observatory for Earth-like Exoplanets (HOEE) concept presents a potential solution by combining an orbiting starshade with a large ground-based telescope to suppress starlight and enable direct imaging of exoplanets.

We have pioneered a transformative approach to the search for life beyond our solar system by deploying a space-borne starshade to cast a near perfect shadow over Earth’s largest telescopes, we suppress stellar glare before it ever enters the atmosphere. John Mather HOEE principal investigator at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland

Recent research, published earlier this year and featured on the cover of Monday’s Nature Astronomy March issue, suggests the HOEE concept could produce much sharper images allowing us to see entire exoplanetary systems and to clearly separate planet images from each other as well as from interference of dust clouds, the host star, and from the starshade itself. Its extreme sensitivity could enable the detection of small planets, and even large dwarf planets. Most notably, it could enable high-fidelity, wide-band spectroscopy, a scientific technique that can be used to study the interaction between matter and light, improving the path to identifying the chemical signatures of life.

For decades, the starshade was a novel concept. Now, NASA’s Innovative Advanced Concepts (NIAC) program is turning that idea into a buildable reality. Through a series of targeted studies, NASA researchers are investigating whether it could be practical to build and develop an engineering roadmap.

Team leading NASA’s Hybrid Observatory for Earth-like Exoplanets concept pictured with the cover of Nature Astronomy featuring their research “The observation of Earth-like exoplanets with ground-based telescopes and a shared orbiting starshade.” From left NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center researchers Dr. John Mather and Dr. Eliad Peretz, followed by NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory researchers Dr. Ahmed Soliman and Dr. Stuart Shaklan. KISS

NASA’s Hybrid Observatory for Earth-like Exoplanets (HOEE) is a three-time NIAC award recipient, having received Phase I awards in 2022 and 2025. The HOEE concept is supported by researchers at NASA Goddard, NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California, and NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley.

Latest Related Research
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological advancement by NASA that could enable the direct imaging and detailed study of Earth-like exoplanets, potentially identifying signs of life beyond our solar system. This represents a meaningful breakthrough in space exploration with broad scientific and inspirational impact. The article provides substantial context about the technology, its challenges, and the collaborative research efforts, fulfilling the criteria for good news.
articles.personality_title: NASA develops new technology to find and study Earth-like planets
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA wants to find planets outside our solar system that are similar to Earth. These planets, called exoplanets, could have water, oxygen, and maybe even signs of life. But it is hard to see them because the stars they orbit are very bright and hide the planets’ faint light.

**What happened** – NASA created a new idea called the Hybrid Observatory for Earth-like Exoplanets (HOEE). It uses a special tool called a starshade that flies in space and blocks the bright light from stars before it reaches big telescopes on Earth. This helps the telescopes see the planets more clearly. Scientists from NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, and Ames Research Center are working together on this project. The idea has won awards and was featured in a science magazine called Nature Astronomy.

**Impact** – This technology could help us take sharp pictures of planets far away and study their atmospheres to find chemicals like water and oxygen. It might even detect small planets and dwarf planets that were too hard to see before. This is important because it brings us closer to understanding if there is life beyond Earth.

**What's next step** – NASA’s Innovative Advanced Concepts program is studying how to build the starshade and make the whole system work. The next steps include creating a plan to develop and test this technology so it can be used in future space missions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s new starshade and telescope system could reveal detailed images and signs of life on planets outside our solar system for the first time.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA développe une nouvelle technologie pour trouver et étudier des planètes semblables à la Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA cherche à découvrir des planètes en dehors de notre système solaire qui ressemblent à la Terre. Ces planètes, appelées exoplanètes, pourraient avoir de l'eau, de l'oxygène, et peut-être même des signes de vie. Mais il est difficile de les voir car les étoiles autour desquelles elles tournent sont très brillantes et cachent la faible lumière des planètes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La NASA a créé une nouvelle idée appelée l'Observatoire Hybride pour Exoplanètes semblables à la Terre (HOEE). Il utilise un outil spécial appelé un écran d'étoile qui vole dans l'espace et bloque la lumière brillante des étoiles avant qu'elle n'atteigne de grands télescopes sur Terre. Cela aide les télescopes à voir les planètes plus clairement. Des scientifiques du Goddard Space Flight Center, du Jet Propulsion Laboratory, et du Ames Research Center de la NASA travaillent ensemble sur ce projet. L'idée a remporté des prix et a été présentée dans une revue scientifique appelée Nature Astronomy.

**Impact** – Cette technologie pourrait nous aider à prendre des images nettes de planètes lointaines et à étudier leur atmosphère pour trouver des éléments comme l'eau et l'oxygène. Elle pourrait même détecter de petites planètes et des planètes naines qui étaient trop difficiles à voir auparavant. C’est important car cela nous rapproche de la compréhension de l’existence possible de la vie au-delà de la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Le programme des Concepts Innovants Avancés de la NASA étudie comment construire l'écran d'étoile et faire fonctionner tout le système. Les prochaines étapes comprennent la création d'un plan pour développer et tester cette technologie afin qu'elle puisse être utilisée dans de futures missions spatiales.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau système de la NASA combinant écran d'étoile et télescope pourrait révéler pour la première fois des images détaillées et des signes de vie sur des planètes en dehors de notre système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA desarrolla nueva tecnología para encontrar y estudiar planetas similares a la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA quiere encontrar planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar que sean parecidos a la Tierra. Estos planetas, llamados exoplanetas, podrían tener agua, oxígeno y quizás señales de vida. Pero es difícil verlos porque las estrellas cercanas son muy brillantes y ocultan la débil luz de los planetas.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA creó una nueva idea llamada Observatorio Híbrido para Exoplanetas semejantes a la Tierra (HOEE). Usa una herramienta especial llamada pantalla estelar que vuela en el espacio y bloquea la luz brillante de las estrellas antes de que llegue a grandes telescopios en la Tierra. Esto ayuda a los telescopios a ver los planetas con más claridad. Científicos del Centro Goddard de la NASA, el Laboratorio de Propulsión a Chorro y el Centro Ames trabajan juntos en este proyecto. La idea ha ganado premios y apareció en una revista científica llamada Nature Astronomy.

**Impacto** – Esta tecnología podría ayudarnos a tomar imágenes nítidas de planetas lejanos y estudiar sus atmósferas para encontrar químicos como agua y oxígeno. Incluso podría detectar planetas pequeños y planetas enanos que antes eran difíciles de ver. Esto es importante porque nos acerca a saber si hay vida fuera de la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – El programa de Conceptos Avanzados Innovadores de la NASA está estudiando cómo construir la pantalla estelar y hacer que todo el sistema funcione. Los próximos pasos incluyen crear un plan para desarrollar y probar esta tecnología para que pueda usarse en futuras misiones espaciales.

**Resumen en una frase** – El nuevo sistema de la NASA con pantalla estelar y telescopios podría mostrar por primera vez imágenes detalladas y señales de vida en planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar.
articles.image_prompt: An intricately detailed painting of a large, elegant telescope on Earth silhouetted against a softly glowing twilight sky, with a sleek, translucent starshade spacecraft casting a precise shadow over the telescope’s aperture, while distant, softly illuminated Earth-like exoplanets with subtle blue and green hues orbit a distant star in the background, all rendered in warm, natural tones conveying hope and discovery.

articles.title: NASA’s Water-Hunting Tool Will Help Scout Moon’s South Pole
articles.summary: NASA is joining international partners to hunt for ice on the Moon in support of future human exploration. The agency is providing a water-detecting instrument, the Neutron Spectrometer System (NSS), to the Lunar Polar Exploration (LUPEX) mission led by JAXA (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) and ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation).
articles.content:
Apollo 17 geologist and astronaut Harrison Schmitt next to a large bolder on the Taurus-Littrow landing site on the Moon. NASA

NASA is joining international partners to hunt for ice on the Moon in support of future human exploration. The agency is providing a water-detecting instrument, the Neutron Spectrometer System (NSS), to the Lunar Polar Exploration (LUPEX) mission led by JAXA (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) and ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation).

The instrument, which detects ice under the lunar surface, will be installed on LUPEX’s lunar rover planned to arrive at the Moon no earlier than 2028. NASA’s support of LUPEX is part of an ongoing effort to identify and characterize lunar water and other materials that easily evaporate near the Moon’s South Pole.

Water is a critical material for NASA’s plans to develop an enduring presence on the Moon. Instead of relying solely on resources carried from Earth, astronauts could use the Moon’s water for breathable air, rocket fuel, and more. The first step is to find deposits of meaningful quantities of water close to the surface to mark potential landing areas for future astronauts. The water on the Moon is mostly found as molecules within lunar regolith, the dusty and rocky material that covers the Moon’s surface, but there may be ice deposits below the surface of the lunar South Pole. Once we better understand the quantity and quality of the available resources, we can learn how to harness it for exploration.

“There is currently a gap in our understanding of how lunar ice is distributed at small scales, from 10s of centimeters up to 10s of kilometers,” said Rick Elphic, NSS lead at NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley, where the instrument was developed in collaboration with Lockheed Martin Advanced Technology Center in Palo Alto, California. “The only way to understand the ‘where’ and ‘how much’ of lunar ice is by exploring on the surface at these scales.”

How neutrons signal water

NASA’s Neutron Spectrometer System instrument will search for signs of water ice on the Moon’s surface aboard a lunar rover belonging to the Lunar Polar Exploration (LUPEX) mission led by JAXA (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) and ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation). NASA/Warren Davis

Scientists can search for water on the Moon without drilling into the surface. Instead, they hunt for concentrations of hydrogen, the H in H₂O. Past missions in lunar orbit have found signs of water at the Moon’s poles, but ground missions are needed to build detailed maps of location and quantity.

Instruments like NSS can infer the presence of hydrogen by detecting interactions with particles called neutrons. Neutrons are constantly rattling around in the lunar soil, and they’re about the same size as hydrogen atoms. When these two particles interact, fewer medium-energy neutrons are ejected from the soil. The absence of medium-energy neutrons suggests more of the particles are interacting with hydrogen underground, a deficit that can be measured with the right tools.

The NSS instrument uses a “gas proportional counter” to detect neutrons bouncing out of the lunar soil. It features two tubes that contain a rare gas called helium-3 that is very sensitive to neutrons. When neutrons strike the helium-3 gas atoms, the gas produces electrical pulses that can be counted to infer the presence and quantity of hydrogen up to three feet underground.

Series of water-hunters

Ongoing investigation of the Moon’s water will inform how astronauts might access it in the future. To that end, NASA researchers at Ames have developed a series of NSS instruments intended to ride aboard different missions to investigate sites at the Moon’s South Pole.

The first Moon-bound NSS instrument in the series was carried aboard Astrobotic’s Peregrine lander, Astrobotic Peregrine Mission One, which launched in January 2024. That mission came to an end without touching down on the lunar surface, but the NSS aboard powered on and operated on multiple days over the course of the 10-day mission. These operations successfully captured data about the particle background of deep space, which strongly supported NSS operations on future missions.

NASA’s VIPER (Volatiles Investigating Polar Exploration Rover) mission, part of the agency’s Artemis campaign, will carry another NSS. As part of NASA’s ongoing Commercial Lunar Payload Services effort, a fourth NSS instrument will ride aboard the MoonRanger “micro rover” developed by Carnegie Mellon University in Pittsburgh.

“The three upcoming NSS rover expeditions will tell us what kinds of places on the Moon are most likely to host ice,” Elphic said. “Missions to the lunar surface can then be planned to similar sites where ice can be found.”

The Neutron Spectrometer System was jointly developed by NASA’s Ames Research Center and Lockheed Martin Advanced Technology Center in Palo Alto, California.

For more information on the science of water on the Moon, visit:

https://science.nasa.gov/moon/moon-water-and-ices

Karen Fox / Molly Wasser

Headquarters, Washington

240-285-5155 / 240-419-1732

karen.c.fox@nasa.gov / molly.l.wasser@nasa.gov

Arezu Sarvestani

Ames Research Center, Silicon Valley

650-613-2334

arezu.sarvestani@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's contribution of a neutron spectrometer instrument to an international lunar mission aimed at detecting water ice on the Moon's South Pole. This has broad significance for future human space exploration by enabling sustainable lunar presence through in-situ resource utilization. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful scientific and technological advancement with tangible benefits for humanity's space exploration goals.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s neutron detector to help find water ice on Moon’s South Pole
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working with Japan’s JAXA and India’s ISRO space agencies on a mission called Lunar Polar Exploration (LUPEX). The goal is to find water ice near the Moon’s South Pole, which is important for future astronauts living and working on the Moon.

**What happened** – NASA is providing a special instrument called the Neutron Spectrometer System (NSS) to LUPEX. This tool will ride on a lunar rover set to arrive no earlier than 2028. NSS detects hydrogen underground by measuring neutrons, which helps locate water ice beneath the Moon’s surface.

**Impact** – Finding water ice on the Moon is important because it can be used for breathing air, rocket fuel, and other needs. Instead of bringing all water from Earth, astronauts could use what they find on the Moon. This mission will give detailed information about where water ice is and how much is there, especially at small scales from a few centimeters to kilometers.

**What's next step** – After the NSS helps map water ice on the Moon, future missions can target these spots to land astronauts and collect resources. NASA is also using similar instruments on other missions like VIPER and MoonRanger to learn more about lunar water.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s neutron detector on the LUPEX rover will help find and measure water ice on the Moon’s South Pole to support future human missions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le détecteur de neutrons de la NASA pour trouver la glace d’eau au pôle Sud de la Lune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA collabore avec les agences spatiales japonaises JAXA et indiennes ISRO sur une mission appelée Lunar Polar Exploration (LUPEX). L’objectif est de trouver de la glace d’eau près du pôle Sud de la Lune, ce qui est important pour les futurs astronautes qui vivront et travailleront sur la Lune.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA fournit un instrument spécial appelé le système de spectromètre à neutrons (NSS) à LUPEX. Cet outil sera installé sur un rover lunaire prévu pour arriver au plus tôt en 2028. Le NSS détecte l’hydrogène sous la surface en mesurant les neutrons, ce qui aide à localiser la glace d’eau sous la surface lunaire.

**Impact** – Trouver de la glace d’eau sur la Lune est important car elle peut être utilisée pour l’air respirable, le carburant des fusées et d’autres besoins. Au lieu d’apporter toute l’eau depuis la Terre, les astronautes pourraient utiliser celle qu’ils trouvent sur la Lune. Cette mission donnera des informations précises sur l’emplacement et la quantité de glace, surtout à petite échelle.

**Prochaine étape** – Une fois que le NSS aura aidé à cartographier la glace d’eau, les prochaines missions pourront cibler ces endroits pour y poser des astronautes et collecter des ressources. La NASA utilise aussi des instruments similaires sur d’autres missions comme VIPER et MoonRanger pour en savoir plus.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le détecteur de neutrons de la NASA sur le rover LUPEX aidera à trouver et mesurer la glace d’eau au pôle Sud de la Lune pour soutenir les futures missions humaines.
articles.personality_title_es: El detector de neutrones de la NASA ayudará a encontrar hielo de agua en el polo sur lunar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA colabora con las agencias espaciales de Japón (JAXA) y la India (ISRO) en una misión llamada Lunar Polar Exploration (LUPEX). El objetivo es buscar hielo de agua cerca del polo sur de la Luna, algo importante para futuros astronautas que vivirán y trabajarán allí.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA está proporcionando un instrumento especial llamado Sistema de Espectrómetro de Neutrones (NSS) para LUPEX. Este equipo irá montado en un rover lunar que llegará no antes de 2028. El NSS detecta hidrógeno bajo la superficie midiendo neutrones, lo que ayuda a encontrar hielo de agua bajo el suelo lunar.

**Impacto** – Encontrar hielo de agua en la Luna es útil porque puede usarse para respirar, combustible de cohetes y otras necesidades. En lugar de llevar toda el agua desde la Tierra, los astronautas podrían utilizar la que encuentren en la Luna. Esta misión dará información detallada sobre dónde está el hielo y cuánto hay, especialmente en escalas pequeñas.

**Próximo paso** – Después de que el NSS ayude a mapear el hielo de agua, futuras misiones podrán aterrizar en esos lugares para que los astronautas recojan recursos. La NASA también usa instrumentos similares en otras misiones como VIPER y MoonRanger para aprender más.

**Resumen en una frase** – El detector de neutrones de la NASA en el rover LUPEX ayudará a encontrar y medir hielo de agua en el polo sur lunar para apoyar futuras misiones humanas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a futuristic lunar rover equipped with twin cylindrical instruments gently scanning the dusty, rocky surface near the Moon’s South Pole, with subtle glimmers beneath the soil hinting at hidden ice deposits, set against a softly glowing Earthrise on the dark horizon under a star-filled sky.
